{"id":"roots-of-reason-e2s","title":"ADR-007: Quality Pipeline — Test-Driven Game Development","description":"Every system in Roots of Reason must be testable and tested before merge. The CI pipeline is not an afterthought — it is a first-class game system that enforces quality gates on every PR.

## Decision

1. **GdUnit4** as the unit/integration test framework (GDScript-native, JUnit XML output, CI-friendly).
2. **Minimum 80% line coverage** on all gameplay scripts (autoloads/, scripts/). Coverage enforced in CI — PRs that drop below threshold are blocked.
3. **Static analysis via gdtoolkit** (gdlint + gdformat) — zero warnings policy. Auto-format on save, lint in CI.
4. **Headless scene smoke tests** — every scene must load without errors in headless mode. CI spins up Godot --headless and validates scene tree instantiation.
5. **Playability integration tests** — automated scenarios that simulate real gameplay: spawn units, gather resources, build buildings, advance ages. These run headless and assert game state correctness.
6. **Screenshot regression testing** — for UI scenes, capture screenshots in CI and compare against baselines. Flag visual regressions for manual review.
7. **Performance budgets** — frame time benchmarks for key scenarios (100 units on screen, full map revealed, late-game economy). CI fails if benchmarks regress beyond threshold.
8. **Export validation** — CI builds macOS and Windows exports and verifies they launch without crash (headless smoke test on exported binaries).

## Consequences

- High confidence in every merge — no "it compiles, ship it"
- Slower CI runs (~5-10 min) but catches regressions immediately
- Test infrastructure is built once and pays dividends on every subsequent epic
- Playability tests serve as living documentation of game mechanics
- Coverage requirement forces testable architecture (dependency injection, data-driven design)
- Screenshot tests catch UI regressions that unit tests miss","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"decision","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:33Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:33Z","labels":["adr","architecture","ci"]}

{"description":"Defines the visual production pipeline for all game assets: tiles, units, buildings, UI elements, effects. This is the single highest-risk bottleneck for a solo/small team building an isometric RTS.

DECISION

1. Tile geometry: 128x64px diamond tiles (2:1 ratio). Elevation step: 32px vertical offset per level (half tile height). 3 usable elevation levels.

2. Sprite scale contract:
   - Villager: ~48px tall (standing), fits within one tile footprint
   - Infantry/military: ~52-56px tall
   - Cavalry/mounted: ~64px tall
   - Buildings (1x1): 128x128px max
   - Buildings (2x2): 256x192px max
   - Buildings (3x3): 384x256px max
   - Trees/resources: ~64-80px tall, single tile footprint

3. Animation budget per unit type (AUTHORING cost, not output count):
   - Idle: 4 keyframes (rendered to 32 output frames via 8 cameras)
   - Walk: 8 keyframes (rendered to 64 output frames)
   - Attack: 6 keyframes (rendered to 48 output frames)
   - Death: 6 keyframes (rendered to 6 output frames, 1 direction mirrored)
   - Gather (villager, per resource): 6 keyframes x 4 types (rendered to 96 output frames each)
   - Military unit authoring cost: ~24 keyframes -> ~150 output frames (6x multiplication via render rig)
   - Villager authoring cost: ~48 keyframes -> ~230 output frames
   The 3D render pipeline eliminates per-direction manual work entirely.

4. Art production method: THREE-PHASE AUTOMATED PIPELINE.

   Phase 1 (prototype) — FULLY PROCEDURAL, ZERO MANUAL ART:
   A Python/GDScript generator creates all placeholder sprites programmatically.
   Colored geometric shapes: diamonds for tiles, circles for units, rectangles for buildings.
   Script generates all directions, animation frames, player color variants.
   Output committed to repo OR generated at build time.
   Game is playable within hours of project creation.

   Phase 2 (alpha) — BLENDER 3D-TO-2D RENDER PIPELINE:
   This is the core production method, modeled after how Age of Empires was actually made.
   (a) Model unit/building once in Blender (low-poly, stylized)
   (b) Rig with standard humanoid skeleton (units) or no rig (buildings)
   (c) Animate once — walk cycle, attack, idle, death. ONE direction only.
   (d) Isometric render rig: 8 cameras at 45-degree intervals, all at iso angle (30 deg from horizontal), orthographic projection, consistent lighting
   (e) Blender script batch-renders all animations from all 8 cameras automatically
   (f) Pipeline script packs rendered PNGs into spritesheets, generates Godot SpriteFrames resources
   (g) Player color variants: render with neutral base, apply team color via shader at runtime (NOT re-rendering per player)
   One animation authored = 8 directions output. One model = all player colors via shader.

   Phase 3 (polish) — TEXTURE REFINEMENT:
   AI-generated textures applied to 3D models, re-rendered through same pipeline.
   Hand-touch-up on final spritesheets in Aseprite for hero units and unique buildings.
   Pipeline stays the same — only the model/texture quality improves.

5. Player color system: Runtime shader recoloring, NOT baked sprites.
   Units and buildings rendered with a designated "team color" mask region.
   A Godot shader swaps the mask color to the player's color at runtime.
   This means ONE set of sprites serves all players — no per-player sprite variants needed.
   Player colors: Blue (#2E86DE), Red (#E74C3C), Teal (#1ABC9C), Orange (#F39C12).

6. Color palettes per age cluster:
   - Stone/Bronze: earthy browns, muted greens, terracotta
   - Iron/Medieval: deeper greens, grays, stone blues
   - Industrial: brick reds, iron grays, smoke, amber
   - Information/Singularity: steel blues, white, neon accents, holographic

7. Tileset production:
   - 5 base terrains, ~15-20 variants each via Godot TileMap terrain autotiling
   - Terrain tiles: procedurally generated for Phase 1, Blender-rendered for Phase 2
   - Total: ~120 tiles minimum viable (80-100 terrain + 20 mountain + 12 elevation)

8. Asset file organization:
   - assets/sprites/units/{unit_name}/ — spritesheet per unit (shared across players via shader)
   - assets/sprites/buildings/{building_name}/ — spritesheet per building
   - assets/tiles/terrain/ — tileset textures
   - assets/ui/ — UI elements, icons, fonts
   - assets/effects/ — particle textures, projectiles
   - blender/ — source .blend files, render rig template, batch export scripts
   - tools/ — procedural generator, spritesheet packer, CI validation scripts

9. Automation toolchain (all scripted, all CI-validated):
   - tools/generate_placeholders.py — generates Phase 1 geometric sprites
   - tools/spritesheet_packer.py — packs rendered PNGs into spritesheets + Godot resources
   - blender/render_rig.blend — template scene with 8 cameras, lighting, render settings
   - blender/batch_render.py — Blender script: render all animations for a model from all cameras
   - CI step: validate sprite dimensions match scale contract, frame counts match budget

CONSEQUENCES

- Phase 1 is FULLY AUTOMATED — zero manual art, game playable immediately
- Phase 2 authoring cost: ~24 keyframes per military unit (not 150 hand-drawn frames)
- 3D pipeline gives 6-8x frame multiplication — 8 directions for the cost of 1
- Player colors handled by shader — no per-player sprite duplication
- Same pipeline serves all 3 phases — only input quality improves
- Blender is free, scriptable, and has excellent isometric rendering support
- CI validates sprite consistency — wrong dimensions caught before merge
- Total authoring cost for 6 unit types: ~200 keyframes (not 1050 hand-drawn frames)"}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-3hk","title":"ADR-008: Art Pipeline — Isometric Asset Production Strategy","description":"Defines the visual production pipeline for all game assets: tiles, units, buildings, UI elements, effects. This is the single highest-risk bottleneck for a solo/small team building an isometric RTS. Every decision here has compounding downstream effects — tile dimensions determine map scale, sprite resolution determines animation cost, and art method determines total throughput.

DECISION

1. Tile geometry: 128x64px diamond tiles (2:1 ratio). Elevation step: 32px vertical offset per level (half tile height). This gives 3 usable elevation levels within reasonable sprite heights.

2. Sprite scale contract:
   - Villager: ~48px tall (standing), fits within one tile footprint
   - Infantry/military: ~52-56px tall
   - Cavalry/mounted: ~64px tall
   - Buildings (1x1): 128x128px max (one tile footprint, extends above tile)
   - Buildings (2x2): 256x192px max (four tile footprint)
   - Buildings (3x3): 384x256px max (Town Center, Wonder)
   - Trees/resources: ~64-80px tall, single tile footprint

3. Animation budget per unit type:
   - Idle: 4 frames x 8 directions = 32 frames
   - Walk: 8 frames x 8 directions = 64 frames
   - Attack: 6 frames x 8 directions = 48 frames
   - Death: 6 frames x 1 direction (mirrored) = 6 frames
   - Gather (villager only, per resource type): 6 frames x 4 directions = 24 frames x 4 = 96 frames
   - Total per military unit: ~150 frames
   - Total for villager: ~230 frames (most expensive unit)
   Buildings: idle (1-2 frames), construction (4 stages), destruction (3 stages), no directional variants.

4. Art production method: Hybrid pipeline.
   - Phase 1 (prototype): Colored geometric shapes. Diamonds for tiles, circles for units, rectangles for buildings. Distinct colors per type. Zero art skill required. Gets the game playable immediately.
   - Phase 2 (alpha): AI-generated base sprites (Stable Diffusion / DALL-E / Midjourney) with manual cleanup in Aseprite. Generate isometric renders, hand-clean edges, ensure palette consistency. Fastest path to "looks like a game."
   - Phase 3 (polish): Hand-pixel or commission professional sprites for hero units, unique buildings, and UI. Replace AI bases where quality falls short.
   This phased approach means art never blocks engineering. Gameplay is always testable with current-phase art.

5. Color palette: 5 palettes — one per age cluster.
   - Stone/Bronze Age: earthy browns, muted greens, terracotta
   - Iron/Medieval Age: deeper greens, grays, stone blues, banners with civ colors
   - Industrial Age: brick reds, iron grays, smoke, amber
   - Information/Singularity Age: steel blues, white, neon accents, holographic highlights
   Each civ has a primary + secondary color applied to units and building banners (not full recoloring — just team markers).

6. Tileset scope (minimum viable for map gen):
   - 5 base terrain types: grass, forest floor, desert/sand, shallow water, deep water
   - Per terrain: center tile + 8 edge transitions to each adjacent type = massive combinatorial explosion
   - Solution: Use Godot 4 TileMap terrain autotiling. Define terrain peering bits. Need ~15-20 tile variants per terrain type to cover edges, corners, and inner corners.
   - Total base tileset: ~80-100 tile variants for 5 terrains
   - Mountain/cliff tiles: separate layer, ~20 variants
   - Elevation transitions: ~12 variants (ramp up, ramp down, cliff edge per cardinal direction)
   - Grand total tileset: ~120 tiles minimum viable

7. Asset file organization:
   - assets/sprites/units/{civ}/{unit_name}/ — spritesheet per unit
   - assets/sprites/buildings/{civ}/{building_name}/ — spritesheet per building
   - assets/tiles/terrain/ — tileset textures
   - assets/tiles/elevation/ — elevation tiles
   - assets/ui/ — UI elements, icons, fonts
   - assets/effects/ — particle textures, projectiles
   - assets/audio/ — sound effects, music (future)
   Naming convention: snake_case, descriptive. E.g., villager_walk_se_03.png

8. Spritesheet format: Pack all directional frames into a single spritesheet per animation. Use Godot SpriteFrames resource for animation definitions. Aseprite as the source-of-truth editor (exports to PNG spritesheet + JSON metadata).

CONSEQUENCES

- Phased art approach means engineering is never blocked by art production
- Geometric placeholders are ugly but functional — game is always playable
- AI-assisted middle phase dramatically accelerates asset production (10x vs hand-drawing)
- 128x64 tile size is generous enough for detail but small enough to fit large maps on screen
- Animation budget is realistic (~150 frames/unit) — not cutting corners but not AAA
- The tileset scope (~120 tiles) is the first major art investment and should be prioritized
- Sprite scale contract prevents the "everything is the wrong size" problem that kills isometric games
- 8-direction sprites are industry standard for isometric; 4 directions looks cheap, 16 is overkill","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"decision","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T20:04:37Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T20:04:37Z","labels":["adr","architecture","art"]}

{"description":"Create a Python script (tools/generate_tiles.py) that procedurally generates the entire Phase 1 placeholder tileset. Zero manual drawing.

The script generates 128x64px isometric diamond tiles using PIL/Pillow:
- Grass: green fill (#4A7C4A) with 1px darker border
- Forest floor: dark green (#3D5A3D) with small triangle shapes suggesting trees
- Desert: tan fill (#D4B87A) with subtle dot pattern
- Shallow water: light blue (#5B9BD5) with wavy line pattern
- Deep water: dark blue (#2E5B8A) with darker wavy lines
- Mountain: gray (#8C8C8C) with triangle peaks
- Elevation ramp: gradient from low to high color

For terrain autotiling, generate transition variants:
- Use Godot 4 TileMap terrain peering bit system
- For each terrain pair, generate edge tiles by splitting the diamond into regions and filling each with the appropriate terrain color
- Generate: center, 4 cardinal edges, 4 corners, 4 inner corners = 13 variants per terrain type
- Total output: ~65-80 tile PNGs

Also generate resource node sprites (32x32 on tile):
- Gold: yellow circle with G label
- Stone: gray hexagon with S label
- Wood: brown rectangle (tree trunk)
- Berry bush: red dots cluster
- Fish: blue fish shape on water
- Deer: brown triangle

Script outputs:
- Individual PNGs to assets/tiles/terrain/
- A Godot .tres TileSet resource file configured with terrain sets and peering bits
- A manifest JSON listing all generated tiles with metadata

Script is idempotent — running it again overwrites with identical output.
Add to CI: regenerate and diff to ensure committed tiles match generator output.

Acceptance criteria:
- python3 tools/generate_tiles.py runs without dependencies beyond Pillow
- Generates 60+ tile PNGs that load correctly in Godot TileMap
- Terrain autotiling produces smooth transitions in editor
- All 5 terrain types + resources visually distinct at game zoom
- CI validates generated output matches committed assets","title":"Procedural tileset generator — Phase 1 terrain tiles"}

Tiles needed (128x64px diamond iso):
- Grass: green diamond
- Forest floor: dark green diamond with small triangle trees
- Desert/sand: tan/yellow diamond
- Shallow water: light blue diamond with simple wave pattern
- Deep water: dark blue diamond

For each terrain, create:
- Center tile (1 variant)
- Edge transitions using Godot 4 TileMap terrain autotiling peering bits
- At minimum: 4 cardinal edges + 4 corner pieces per terrain pair
- Use solid color fills with 1px darker border lines to distinguish tiles

Also create:
- Mountain/cliff placeholder: gray triangles on tile
- Elevation ramp placeholder: angled gradient tile
- Resource node placeholders: colored circles (gold=yellow, stone=gray, wood=brown, food=red)

Configure Godot TileSet resource with:
- Isometric tile size 128x64
- Terrain sets defined with autotile peering
- Physics layers for walkability (water=impassable, mountain=impassable)

Target: ~40-60 placeholder tiles that let map generation produce a visually distinguishable map.

Acceptance criteria:
- TileSet resource loads in Godot editor without errors
- All 5 terrain types visually distinct at game zoom level
- Terrain autotiling produces reasonable transitions
- Resource nodes distinguishable from terrain","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:18Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:18Z","labels":["art","story","tiles"]}

{"description":"Create a Python script (tools/generate_unit_sprites.py) that procedurally generates all Phase 1 placeholder unit sprites. Zero manual drawing.

The script generates sprites using PIL/Pillow for each unit type:

Unit definitions (shape, base size):
- Villager: circle, 24px diameter, carry-bag indicator when gathering
- Infantry: circle with flat bottom (shield), 26px
- Archer: circle with line extending outward (bow), 26px
- Cavalry: horizontal oval, 32x20px
- Siege: rectangle, 28x20px
- Naval: wide diamond, 36x16px

For each unit, generate:
- 8 directional variants (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) — same shape with rotated direction arrow/indicator
- Idle animation: 2 frames (subtle size pulse) x 8 dirs = 16 frames
- Walk animation: 4 frames (bouncing motion via vertical offset) x 8 dirs = 32 frames
- Attack animation: 3 frames (flash + extend) x 8 dirs = 24 frames
- Death animation: 4 frames (shrink + fade) x 1 dir = 4 frames
- Total per unit: ~76 frames (simplified from 150 — Phase 1 uses fewer frames)

Player color: render with NEUTRAL color (magenta #FF00FF) as the team-color mask region.
The runtime shader will replace this with actual player color.
Also generate a preview set with Player 1 blue for visual testing.

Selection indicator: separate green circle outline sprite.
Health bar: separate sprite (green/yellow/red segments).

Output:
- Individual PNGs organized per unit: assets/sprites/units/placeholder/{unit_name}/
- Packed spritesheets per unit (one PNG + JSON frame data)
- Godot SpriteFrames .tres resource per unit with named animations
- Manifest JSON listing all units, frame counts, dimensions

Acceptance criteria:
- python3 tools/generate_unit_sprites.py generates all 6 unit types
- Each unit visually distinct from others at game zoom
- Direction arrow makes facing obvious
- Magenta team-color region clearly defined for shader swap
- SpriteFrames resources load and preview correctly in Godot editor
- CI validates output matches committed sprites","title":"Procedural unit sprite generator — Phase 1 placeholder units"}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-be7.2","title":"Create Phase 1 geometric placeholder unit sprites","description":"Create placeholder unit sprites using colored geometric shapes. These are functional stand-ins that make the game testable without real art.

Units needed:
- Villager: small colored circle (~24px diameter) with player color fill, tiny direction indicator (triangle arrow showing facing)
- Infantry: slightly larger circle with square shield icon
- Archer: circle with line indicating range
- Cavalry: oval (elongated, suggesting mounted)
- Siege: large rectangle
- Naval/ship: diamond shape (wider than tall)

For each unit create:
- 8-direction facing variants (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) — can be the same circle with a rotated direction arrow
- Idle: 1 frame per direction (just the shape)
- Walk: 2 frames per direction (alternate slight offset for bounce effect)
- Attack: 2 frames per direction (shape + flash)
- Death: 3 frames (shrink to nothing)

Player colors: Player 1 = blue, Player 2 = red, Player 3 = green, Player 4 = yellow. Applied as fill color.

Selection indicator: green circle outline drawn beneath selected units.

Configure as Godot SpriteFrames resources with named animations (idle_n, idle_ne, walk_s, attack_e, etc.).

Acceptance criteria:
- All unit types visually distinct from each other at game zoom
- Direction of movement/facing is clear
- Player ownership is immediately obvious via color
- SpriteFrames resources load and animate in editor preview","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:18Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:18Z","labels":["art","story","units"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-be7.7","title":"Build throwaway isometric feel prototype","description":"Build a quick throwaway prototype (separate scene, not production code) to validate the isometric feel before building real systems. This is a time-boxed spike — max 1 day of effort.

Prototype must answer these questions:
1. Does 128x64 tile size feel right at target zoom? Can you see enough of the map?
2. Does the camera pan/zoom feel responsive? What are good min/max zoom limits?
3. Is a villager at ~48px tall visible and selectable without squinting?
4. Does unit movement speed feel right? How many tiles per second is good?
5. Can you visually distinguish 3-4 different terrain types at game zoom?
6. Does the isometric click-to-world coordinate conversion feel accurate?

Build:
- Hand-place a 20x20 isometric tilemap with 3-4 terrain types using placeholder tiles
- Add a Camera2D with pan (WASD) and zoom (scroll wheel)
- Place 5-10 unit placeholders (colored circles)
- Click to select, right-click to move (basic, no pathfinding — just lerp to position)
- Isometric screen-to-world coordinate conversion for click targeting

Document findings in a short writeup:
- What tile size / zoom range felt good
- What unit speed felt good (tiles per second)
- Any surprises about iso coordinate math
- Screenshots at min zoom, default zoom, max zoom

This prototype is explicitly throwaway — it does NOT need tests, lint, or coverage. It informs ADR decisions before real implementation begins.

Acceptance criteria:
- Can pan and zoom around a 20x20 iso map
- Can click to select and right-click to move a unit
- Findings documented with specific numbers (zoom range, speed, etc.)
- Prototype clearly marked as throwaway (separate directory or branch)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:21Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T20:06:21Z","labels":["art","prototype","story"]}

old_value:
{"id":"roots-of-reason-mgt.1","title":"Integrate GdUnit4 test framework with project","description":"Install and configure GdUnit4 (https://github.com/MikeSchulworking/gdUnit4) as the test framework.

Acceptance criteria:
- GdUnit4 addon installed in addons/gdUnit4/
- Test directory structure: tests/ mirroring scripts/ (e.g., tests/autoloads/test_game_manager.gd)
- Sample test file that passes: test instantiating a Node, asserting a property
- Can run tests locally via Godot editor and via CLI (godot --headless --script)
- Test runner outputs JUnit XML for CI consumption
- Document test conventions in CONTRIBUTING.md (naming, file layout, assertion style)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:35Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:35Z","labels":["ci","story","testing"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-mgt.2","title":"Set up GitHub Actions CI with Godot headless runner","description":"Create .github/workflows/ci.yml that runs on every push and PR.

Pipeline stages (in order):
1. Checkout + cache Godot binary
2. Import project (godot --headless --import)
3. Run gdlint (static analysis)
4. Run gdformat --check (formatting)
5. Run GdUnit4 tests with coverage
6. Export builds (macOS + Windows)
7. Upload build artifacts

Use official Godot Linux headless binary. Pin exact Godot version (e.g., 4.3-stable).
Cache .godot/imported/ for faster reimports.
Fail fast on lint/format, don't waste time on tests if code is malformatted.

Acceptance criteria:
- CI passes on clean main branch
- CI fails when a test fails
- CI fails when lint/format fails
- Build artifacts downloadable from Actions UI","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:36Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:36Z","labels":["ci","github-actions","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-mgt.4","title":"PR quality gate — required checks and branch protection","description":"Configure GitHub branch protection and required status checks.

Requirements:
- Branch protection on main: require PR, require CI pass, require 1 approval (or auto-approve for solo dev)
- Required status checks: lint, format, tests, coverage threshold, export builds
- No direct push to main
- PR template (.github/pull_request_template.md) with checklist:
  [ ] Tests added/updated for changes
  [ ] Coverage threshold met
  [ ] No lint warnings
  [ ] Playability tests pass
  [ ] Screenshots updated (if UI changed)
- Auto-label PRs based on path (e.g., scripts/combat/ → 'combat' label)
- Status checks must pass before merge button is enabled

This is the enforcement layer that makes all other CI work meaningful.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:37Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:53:37Z","labels":["branch-protection","ci","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-mgt.6","title":"Configure gdtoolkit for linting and formatting","description":"Install and configure gdtoolkit (gdlint + gdformat) for static analysis. Create .gdlintrc config file with project conventions (max line length, naming rules). Create .gdformatrc for auto-formatting. gdlint runs in CI and blocks PRs with warnings. gdformat --check runs in CI and blocks unformatted code. Zero warnings policy: no suppressions without code comment explaining why. gdtoolkit is pip-installable.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:54:02Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:54:02Z","labels":["ci","lint","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-mgt.7","title":"Headless scene smoke tests — validate all scenes load","description":"Create a test harness that instantiates every .tscn scene file in headless mode and verifies no errors. Script discovers all .tscn files in scenes/. For each scene: instantiate, add to tree, wait one frame, verify no errors in log. Catches: missing dependencies, broken node references, invalid resource paths, script errors. Runs as part of CI test suite. New scenes automatically included. This is the cheapest high-value test.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:54:09Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:54:09Z","labels":["ci","smoke-test","story"]}

{"description":"Implement Camera2D with: WASD/arrow key panning, mouse wheel zoom (min/max limits), edge-of-screen scrolling, middle-mouse drag pan. Camera should work with isometric coordinate system.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Camera2D node centered on the isometric map
- [ ] WASD/arrow key panning at configurable speed (data/settings/camera.json)
- [ ] Edge-scroll when cursor within 20px of viewport edge (configurable threshold)
- [ ] Mouse wheel zoom with min/max bounds (e.g., 0.5x to 2.0x)
- [ ] Middle-mouse-drag panning as alternative input
- [ ] Camera position clamped to map bounds (no scrolling into void)
- [ ] Zoom centers on mouse cursor position, not screen center
- [ ] All speed/threshold/bounds values loaded from JSON, never hardcoded
- [ ] Camera state serializable (position, zoom) for save/load
- [ ] Smooth interpolation on all movement (no jittery snapping)
- [ ] Unit tests for clamp logic and zoom math
- [ ] Works at 1920x1080 and scales with viewport stretch mode"}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-317.1","title":"Isometric camera with pan, zoom, edge-scroll","description":"Implement Camera2D with: WASD/arrow key panning, mouse wheel zoom (min/max limits), edge-of-screen scrolling, middle-mouse drag pan. Camera should work with isometric coordinate system.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Camera2D node centered on the isometric map
- [ ] WASD/arrow key panning at configurable speed (data/settings/camera.json)
- [ ] Edge-scroll when cursor within 20px of viewport edge (configurable threshold)
- [ ] Mouse wheel zoom with min/max bounds (e.g., 0.5x to 2.0x)
- [ ] Middle-mouse-drag panning as alternative input
- [ ] Camera position clamped to map bounds (no scrolling into void)
- [ ] Zoom centers on mouse cursor position, not screen center
- [ ] All speed/threshold/bounds values loaded from JSON, never hardcoded
- [ ] Camera state serializable (position, zoom) for save/load
- [ ] Smooth interpolation on all movement (no jittery snapping)
- [ ] Unit tests for clamp logic and zoom math
- [ ] Works at 1920x1080 and scales with viewport stretch mode","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:15Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:56:58Z","labels":["camera","core","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-317.2","title":"Unit selection: click, box-select, control groups","description":"Left-click to select single unit, click-drag for box selection, Shift+click to add/remove from selection, Ctrl+number to set control group, number key to recall group. Selection highlight/indicator on units.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Left-click selects a single unit (deselects previous)
- [ ] Left-click on empty ground deselects all
- [ ] Click-drag draws a selection rectangle, selects all owned units within
- [ ] Selection rectangle renders visually (semi-transparent green or team color)
- [ ] Shift+click adds/removes individual unit from selection
- [ ] Ctrl+[0-9] assigns selection to control group
- [ ] [0-9] recalls control group (double-tap centers camera on group)
- [ ] Selected units show visual indicator (circle, highlight, or outline)
- [ ] Cannot select enemy units (click on enemy shows info only)
- [ ] Selection state serializable for save/load
- [ ] Maximum selection size: 40 units (configurable in JSON)
- [ ] Unit tests for box-select geometry (isometric coordinate conversion)
- [ ] Unit tests for control group assign/recall logic","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:16Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:57:03Z","labels":["core","selection","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-317.3","title":"Unit movement with A* pathfinding on isometric grid","description":"Implement A* pathfinding on isometric tile grid. Right-click to move selected units. Handle terrain obstacles, unit collision avoidance. Use Godot's AStarGrid2D or custom implementation suited to iso grid.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Right-click on ground issues move command to selected units
- [ ] A* pathfinding on isometric tile grid using Godot's AStar2D or AStarGrid2D
- [ ] Terrain types affect movement cost (e.g., road=0.5, grass=1.0, forest=1.5, river=2.0)
- [ ] Movement costs loaded from data/settings/terrain.json
- [ ] Units navigate around impassable tiles (water, cliffs, buildings)
- [ ] Group movement: units move in formation, not single-file through one path
- [ ] Move command shows click feedback (ground marker or ping animation)
- [ ] Unit plays walk animation while moving, idle animation when stopped
- [ ] Unit faces movement direction (8-direction sprite facing)
- [ ] Path recalculates if obstacle appears mid-move (building placed, etc.)
- [ ] Movement speed per unit type loaded from JSON unit stats
- [ ] Unit position serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for A* path computation on known grid layouts
- [ ] Unit tests for terrain cost lookups
- [ ] Integration test: unit moves from A to B avoiding obstacles","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:17Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:57:10Z","labels":["core","pathfinding","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-317.5","title":"Game clock and pause/speed controls","description":"Game timer with pause (Space), normal speed, fast (2x), very fast (3x). Display game time in HUD. All game logic should use game delta time, not real time.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Game clock tracks elapsed time in game-seconds (not wall-clock)
- [ ] Pause/unpause with spacebar (configurable keybind)
- [ ] Speed controls: 1x (normal), 1.5x, 2x, 3x (configurable in JSON)
- [ ] Speed control via +/- keys and UI buttons
- [ ] Game clock displayed in HUD (MM:SS format)
- [ ] All game systems use game clock delta, not Engine.delta (respects pause/speed)
- [ ] Pausing freezes all unit movement, gathering, construction, and combat
- [ ] UI remains interactive while paused (can issue commands, queue actions)
- [ ] Game clock value serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for clock tick math at different speed multipliers
- [ ] Visual indicator for current speed (1x/2x/3x shown in HUD)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:18Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:57:21Z","labels":["clock","core","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-1oo.6","title":"ror validate-assets — CI-ready asset validator","description":"Validates all sprites against ADR-008 conventions: dimensions match entity type, naming is snake_case, frame counts match animation budget, magenta mask regions present for player-colored sprites.

{"id":"roots-of-reason-7wg.6","title":"River resource transport system with barges","description":"Implement the river transport system: resources deposited at upstream River Docks are automatically shipped downstream via barges.

Core transport logic:
1. When a villager deposits resources at a River Dock, check for a valid downstream route:
   - Trace river tiles following flow_direction from the dock's adjacent river tile
   - Look for the nearest downstream building that is a valid drop-off (another River Dock owned by same player, or a Town Center within 2 tiles of the river)
   - If found, initiate a barge shipment
   - If no downstream depot exists, resources just stay in the player stockpile as normal (the River Dock still functions as a regular drop-off)

2. Barge entity:
   - Visual: small sprite moving along river tiles following flow_direction
   - Speed: 3x base villager movement speed (configurable via river_dock.json transport_speed_multiplier)
   - Capacity: 30 resources per trip (configurable via max_barge_capacity)
   - Dispatch interval: every 5 seconds, if resources are waiting at the dock (configurable via barge_spawn_interval)
   - Batching: barge carries up to capacity of mixed resource types deposited since last dispatch

3. Resource flow accounting:
   - When barge departs: resources are already in player stockpile (deposited by villager at dock)
   - The barge is purely visual + vulnerability — resources are not "held" by the barge for accounting purposes
   - HOWEVER: if the barge is destroyed, the carried resource amount is DEDUCTED from the player stockpile
   - This creates risk: fast transport but vulnerable to raiding
   - Display "in transit" amount in resource UI as a secondary indicator

4. Barge destruction:
   - Barges have 15 HP and 0 defense (very fragile)
   - Any unit can attack a barge (melee units must be adjacent to river, ranged can shoot from bank)
   - When destroyed: carried resources are lost (deducted from player stockpile), small splash visual
   - Destroyed barges do not respawn automatically — the dock simply dispatches a new one on its next cycle

5. Pathfinding for barges:
   - Barges follow river tiles via flow_direction — no pathfinding needed, just follow the chain
   - At river merges (two rivers join), barge continues downstream on the merged river
   - If the downstream river is blocked (enemy building on bank? destroyed bridge?), barge queues at the blockage

6. Multiple docks on same river:
   - Each dock independently dispatches barges to the nearest downstream depot
   - A chain of docks acts like a relay: Dock A ships to Dock B, Dock B ships to Town Center
   - Each hop is a separate barge

Data file: data/buildings/river_dock.json
{
  "transport_speed_multiplier": 3.0,
  "max_barge_capacity": 30,
  "barge_spawn_interval": 5.0,
  "barge_hp": 15
}

Acceptance criteria:
- Resources deposited at upstream River Dock are visibly transported downstream
- Barges follow river flow direction correctly
- Barges arrive at nearest downstream drop-off and resources remain in stockpile
- Barges can be attacked and destroyed, causing resource loss
- Multiple docks on the same river chain correctly
- Transport speed is noticeably faster than villager walking
- System handles edge cases: dock destroyed mid-transit, no downstream depot, river with no docks","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:01:20Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:01:20Z","labels":["resources","river","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-9r1","title":"Establish error handling conventions (ADR-012)","description":"No project-wide error handling conventions exist. Agents will invent patterns independently unless standardized.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] ADR-012 added to AGENTS.md covering error handling patterns
- [ ] Define pattern for recoverable errors (push_warning + fallback value)
- [ ] Define pattern for unrecoverable errors (push_error + assert in debug)
- [ ] Define pattern for data validation (validate at load time, trust at runtime)
- [ ] Define pattern for missing data files (graceful degradation vs hard fail)
- [ ] Define signal-based error propagation for gameplay events (e.g., path_not_found)
- [ ] Define logging conventions (what to log, when, severity levels)
- [ ] At least 2 code examples (WRONG/RIGHT style matching existing ADR format)
- [ ] Existing autoloads (GameManager, ResourceManager, DataLoader) updated to follow conventions","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:09Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:09Z","labels":["adr","architecture","conventions","task"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-7y5.3","title":"Core buildings: Town Center, House, Farm, Barracks, Dock, Market, Library, Wonder","description":"Implement core building types with stats: Town Center (villager production, drop-off, pop+5), House (pop+5), Farm (food source), Barracks (military units), Dock (ships), Market (trade), Library (research/knowledge), Wonder (win condition). Each has cost, build time, HP.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] 8 building types defined in data/buildings/*.json with: cost, build_time, hp, footprint, age_requirement
- [ ] Town Center: trains villagers, resource drop-off, supports 5 population, garrison capacity 15
- [ ] House: +5 population capacity, no other function
- [ ] Farm: infinite slow food source (villager works farm, not a node), food rate in JSON
- [ ] Barracks: trains Infantry, Archer, Cavalry (unit roster per age in JSON)
- [ ] Dock: trains naval units, naval repair, water resource drop-off
- [ ] Market: resource trading (buy/sell at fluctuating rates per JSON)
- [ ] Library: generates Knowledge per second (rate in JSON), required for tech research
- [ ] Wonder: triggers Wonder victory countdown (10 min), extremely expensive
- [ ] Each building has Phase 1 placeholder sprite (colored rectangle with label)
- [ ] All stats and costs data-driven from JSON — zero hardcoded values
- [ ] Building data loaded via DataLoader.get_building_data()
- [ ] Unit tests for building data loading and validation","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:26Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:49Z","labels":["buildings","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fxt.1","title":"Age advancement: 7 ages with resource costs and prerequisites","description":"Advance through 7 ages at Town Center. Each age requires resource cost (escalating) plus prerequisite buildings. Stone→Bronze: 500F. Bronze→Iron: 800F 200G. Escalating through ages. Advancement takes time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 7 ages defined in data/tech/ages.json: Stone, Bronze, Iron, Medieval, Industrial, Information, Singularity
- [ ] Each age has: resource_cost (dict of resource amounts), prerequisite_buildings (list), research_time
- [ ] Age advancement researched at Town Center
- [ ] Research begins when player clicks "Advance" and can afford cost
- [ ] Progress bar shown during advancement research
- [ ] On completion: age_advanced signal emitted, new buildings/units/techs unlocked
- [ ] Visual feedback: age name displayed prominently, brief screen effect
- [ ] Cannot research next age while current advancement is in progress
- [ ] Age state serializable (current age, advancement progress if in-progress)
- [ ] All costs and prerequisites data-driven from JSON
- [ ] Unit tests for prerequisite checking logic
- [ ] Unit tests for age unlock cascading (what becomes available per age)
- [ ] Integration test: advance from Stone to Bronze, verify unlocks","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:31Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T23:00:04Z","labels":["ages","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-7y5.5","title":"Population cap tied to houses","description":"Each House adds +5 population cap. Town Center adds +5. Maximum population cap of 200. Cannot produce units when at cap. Display current/max in resource bar.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Each House provides +5 population capacity (value in JSON)
- [ ] Town Center provides +5 population capacity (value in JSON)
- [ ] Population cap = sum of all completed housing buildings' capacity
- [ ] Current population = count of all living units (villagers + military)
- [ ] Cannot train new units when current population >= population cap
- [ ] "Need more houses" warning when population is within 2 of cap
- [ ] Training queue paused (not cancelled) when pop cap reached mid-queue
- [ ] Destroying a House reduces cap — if now over cap, existing units remain but no new training
- [ ] Population displayed in resource bar as "current/cap" (e.g., 14/20)
- [ ] Population state serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for cap calculation, over-cap edge cases
- [ ] Integration test: build house → train villager → verify cap respected","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:27Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:52Z","labels":["buildings","population","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fxt.2","title":"Per-age technology research","description":"Each age unlocks a set of technologies researchable at various buildings. Techs provide: unit stat bonuses, economic bonuses, building unlocks. Research costs resources and time. Cannot research techs from future ages.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tech tree defined in data/tech/tech_tree.json organized by age
- [ ] Each tech has: id, name, age, cost, research_time, prerequisites (other techs), effects
- [ ] Effects are typed: stat_modifier, unlock_unit, unlock_building, economic_bonus, etc.
- [ ] Research performed at Library building (one tech at a time per Library)
- [ ] Research queue: max 1 in progress + up to 3 queued per Library (configurable)
- [ ] Tech effects applied immediately on completion via signal
- [ ] Tech tree UI shows available/researched/locked techs (Phase 1: simple list)
- [ ] Researched techs tracked per player for Knowledge Burning system
- [ ] Cannot research tech if prerequisites not met or age requirement not reached
- [ ] Research state serializable (researched techs list, in-progress tech, queue)
- [ ] Unit tests for prerequisite resolution
- [ ] Unit tests for effect application (stat modifiers, unlocks)
- [ ] Integration test: research tech → verify effect applied to existing units","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:31Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T23:00:11Z","labels":["research","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fxt.6","title":"Tech research history tracking and regression engine","description":"Implement an ordered research history for each player and the engine to revert technologies.

Research history:
- Each player maintains an ordered Array of researched tech IDs (chronological order)
- When a tech is researched, append its ID to the history
- Age advancements are tracked separately (not in the tech history array) — they are immune to regression
- History is saved/loaded as part of game state serialization

Tech regression engine:
- Function: regress_latest_tech(player_id: int) -> Dictionary
  - Pops the last entry from the player's research history
  - Returns the tech data (for notifications/visuals)
  - If history is empty, returns null (no tech to lose)

- Function: revert_tech_effects(player_id: int, tech_id: String) -> void
  - Removes the tech's bonuses from all of the player's units, buildings, and economy
  - For stat bonuses (e.g., +2 attack): subtract from all current units of affected type
  - For economic multipliers (e.g., +15% farm output): recalculate all affected production rates
  - For building unlocks: set the building type as unavailable for new construction (existing buildings remain)
  - For unit unlocks: set the unit type as unavailable for new production (existing units remain)
  - Emit signal: tech_regressed(player_id, tech_id, tech_data)

- Function: can_research_tech(player_id: int, tech_id: String) -> bool
  - Existing function, but must now also return true for previously-researched-then-lost techs
  - A lost tech becomes available for re-research if prerequisites are still met

Tech data model additions:
- Add to each player state: research_history: Array[String] (ordered tech IDs)
- Add to tech data: is_researched_by: Dictionary[int, bool] (player_id -> researched)
- When tech is regressed: is_researched_by[player_id] = false, and remove from history

Data file: data/settings/knowledge_burning.json
{
  "enabled": true,
  "trigger": "town_center_destroyed",
  "tech_loss_count": 1,
  "protect_age_advancements": true,
  "protect_first_n_techs": 0,
  "re_research_cost_multiplier": 1.0,
  "cooldown_seconds": 0
}

Acceptance criteria:
- Research history correctly tracks order of all tech research per player
- regress_latest_tech removes the most recent tech and reverts all its effects
- Unit stat bonuses correctly removed from all existing units
- Economic bonuses correctly recalculated
- Building/unit unlocks correctly reverted (existing instances preserved, new production blocked)
- Lost tech can be re-researched at full cost
- Age advancements are never regressed
- Settings from knowledge_burning.json are respected
- All functions have unit tests with 90%+ coverage","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:35Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:35Z","labels":["knowledge-burning","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-2hj.1","title":"Unit stats: HP, attack, defense, speed, range, LOS","description":"Define unit stat model: HP (hit points), Attack (base damage), Defense (damage reduction), Speed (tiles/sec), Range (attack range in tiles), LOS (line of sight radius). Stats loaded from data files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unit stat schema: hp, attack, defense, speed, range, line_of_sight, armor_type, attack_type
- [ ] All unit stats defined in data/units/*.json per unit type
- [ ] Stats loaded via DataLoader.get_unit_stats(unit_id)
- [ ] Stats can be modified by age upgrades (base_stat + upgrade_bonus pattern)
- [ ] Stat modifiers from civ bonuses applied at load time
- [ ] Runtime stat access via unit.get_stat(stat_name) method
- [ ] Stats serializable for save/load (base + current with modifiers)
- [ ] Unit tests for stat loading, modifier stacking, and civ bonus application
- [ ] Validation: all unit JSON files checked for required fields at load time","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:28Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:59:24Z","labels":["stats","story","units"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-2hj.2","title":"Unit types: Villager, Infantry, Archer, Cavalry, Siege, Naval","description":"Implement unit categories: Villager (gathers, builds), Infantry (melee, slow, armored), Archer (ranged, fragile), Cavalry (fast melee, bonus vs archers), Siege (slow, anti-building), Naval (water-only). Each with base stats.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 6 base unit categories defined in data/units/
- [ ] Villager: gathers resources, builds, repairs, low combat stats
- [ ] Infantry: melee, medium HP/attack, bonus vs Archers
- [ ] Archer: ranged, low HP, bonus vs Cavalry, minimum range (can't fire point-blank)
- [ ] Cavalry: fast, high attack, bonus vs Infantry, weak to Archers
- [ ] Siege: slow, high building damage (ignores 80% building reduction), fragile vs units
- [ ] Naval: water-only movement, ranged attack, transports land units
- [ ] Each unit type has Phase 1 placeholder sprite (colored circle with letter: V, I, A, C, S, N)
- [ ] Unit type determines available commands (e.g., only Villagers can gather/build)
- [ ] All stats per unit type data-driven from JSON
- [ ] Civ-specific unique units inherit from base type with stat overrides
- [ ] Unit tests for type-specific command availability
- [ ] Unit tests for bonus damage application (rock-paper-scissors)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:28Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:59:33Z","labels":["story","units"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fxt.8","title":"War research bonus implementation","description":"Implement escalating war research bonus that activates when military units are in combat. Bonus scales by age (5% Stone to 40% Information, 25% Singularity). Lingers 30s after combat ends. Military tech spillovers apply permanent civilian bonuses. Settings in data/settings/research.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] War bonus detected when at least 1 military unit is in combat
- [ ] Bonus value scales by current age per research.json
- [ ] 30-second linger timer after last combat ends
- [ ] Military tech spillovers (e.g. Rifling → Mining +15%) applied permanently on research
- [ ] War bonus multiplied into research speed formula
- [ ] Unit tests for war bonus activation, linger, and expiration
- [ ] Integration test: enter combat → verify research speed increases","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T23:25:39Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T23:25:39Z","labels":["combat","research","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-2hj.3","title":"Unit production from buildings with queue","description":"Buildings produce units: select building, click unit icon, unit added to queue. Queue shows up to 5 units. Production takes time and costs resources (deducted on queue). Units spawn at rally point.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Buildings that produce units have a production queue (max 5 items, configurable in JSON)
- [ ] Queue displays in building info panel when building is selected
- [ ] Each unit has a train_time and cost defined in JSON
- [ ] Training begins when building is idle and queue is non-empty
- [ ] Progress bar shown for current training unit
- [ ] Trained unit spawns at rally point (default: building entrance, relocatable by right-click)
- [ ] Training paused if population cap reached (resumes when cap freed)
- [ ] Right-click queue item to cancel (refunds full cost)
- [ ] Shift+click to queue 5 of same unit at once
- [ ] Queue state serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for queue operations (add, remove, cancel, refund)
- [ ] Unit tests for pop cap pause/resume logic
- [ ] Integration test: queue 3 infantry → verify 3 units spawn sequentially","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:29Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:59:41Z","labels":["production","story","units"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-ovr.2","title":"Perlin noise terrain generation","description":"Use FastNoiseLite (Godot built-in) to generate terrain. Multiple noise layers for elevation, moisture, temperature. Map these to terrain types. Configurable map size (small/medium/large).

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Procedural map generation using FastNoiseLite (Godot built-in)
- [ ] Noise layers: elevation (terrain type), moisture (forest distribution), resource richness
- [ ] Elevation thresholds determine terrain: water < 0.3, sand < 0.4, grass < 0.7, stone < 0.85, mountain >= 0.85
- [ ] Thresholds configurable in data/settings/map_generation.json
- [ ] Seed-based generation (same seed = same map)
- [ ] Seed stored for save/load reproducibility
- [ ] Generated maps have at least 2 landmasses connected by land bridges or shallows
- [ ] Map edges are water (island-style) to provide natural bounds
- [ ] Generation completes in < 2 seconds for large (256x256) maps
- [ ] Unit tests for threshold-to-terrain mapping
- [ ] Integration test: generate map with known seed, verify terrain distribution","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:19Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:08Z","labels":["generation","map","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-ovr.3","title":"Resource node placement on map","description":"Place resource nodes during map gen: gold mines (mountains), stone quarries (hills), forests (wood), berry bushes/deer (food), fish (water). Ensure fair distribution relative to starting positions.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Resource nodes: Berry Bush (Food), Tree (Wood), Stone Mine (Stone), Gold Mine (Gold)
- [ ] Placement uses noise-based distribution (not purely random)
- [ ] Trees cluster near forest terrain, stone/gold near mountains, berries near grass
- [ ] Minimum spacing between same-type resource clusters (configurable in JSON)
- [ ] Each resource node has a finite yield amount (loaded from JSON)
- [ ] Resource nodes are visible on map as distinct sprites (Phase 1: colored shapes)
- [ ] Starting positions guaranteed to have balanced nearby resources
- [ ] Resource node state serializable (remaining yield, position)
- [ ] Resource placement density configurable per map size in JSON
- [ ] Unit tests for placement algorithm (minimum spacing, terrain affinity)
- [ ] Integration test: generate map and verify resource balance near spawn points","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:20Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:13Z","labels":["map","resources","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fk5.1","title":"Attack-move and auto-targeting","description":"Attack-move command: unit moves to target location, engages any enemy in range along the way. Idle military units auto-target nearby enemies within aggro range. Prioritize: attacking unit > closest enemy.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A-click (attack-move): unit moves toward target location, attacks first enemy encountered
- [ ] Auto-targeting: idle military units automatically engage enemies within their LOS range
- [ ] Target priority: lowest HP enemy first (configurable priority in JSON)
- [ ] Units stop to attack, then resume move command after target killed
- [ ] Patrol command (shift-A-click): unit moves between two points, engaging enemies en route
- [ ] Units do not auto-attack while under explicit move command (only attack-move)
- [ ] Stance system: aggressive (auto-engage), defensive (only if attacked), stand ground (no auto)
- [ ] Default stance per unit type in JSON (military: aggressive, villager: defensive)
- [ ] Stance serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for target priority sorting
- [ ] Unit tests for stance behavior (engage vs ignore)
- [ ] Integration test: attack-move through enemy units, verify engagement","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:34Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:59:49Z","labels":["combat","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-7wg.1","title":"Define 5 core resources: Food, Wood, Stone, Gold, Knowledge","description":"Create resource data model with 5 types: Food, Wood, Stone, Gold, Knowledge. Each player has a stockpile. Resources are integers. Knowledge is a unique resource earned from Libraries and research.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Resource enum/constants: FOOD, WOOD, STONE, GOLD, KNOWLEDGE
- [ ] Resource configuration in data/resources/resource_config.json
- [ ] Each resource has: id, display_name, icon_path, color, starting_amount
- [ ] Starting amounts per resource defined per difficulty in JSON
- [ ] Knowledge is NOT gathered from map — generated by buildings (Library, Ziggurat) and research
- [ ] ResourceManager autoload tracks current stockpile per resource per player
- [ ] ResourceManager emits signals: resource_changed(player_id, resource_type, old_amount, new_amount)
- [ ] ResourceManager.can_afford(player_id, cost: Dictionary) -> bool
- [ ] ResourceManager.spend(player_id, cost: Dictionary) -> bool (returns false if insufficient)
- [ ] All amounts stored as integers (no floating-point resource bugs)
- [ ] Resource state serializable for save/load
- [ ] Unit tests for can_afford, spend, and signal emission","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:21Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:58:19Z","labels":["resources","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fk5.2","title":"Melee vs ranged damage resolution","description":"Melee units must be adjacent to attack. Ranged units attack from distance. Damage = attacker.attack - defender.defense (minimum 1). Attack speed varies by unit. Ranged units have projectile travel time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Damage formula: max(1, attacker.attack - defender.defense) per hit
- [ ] Melee units: attack on contact, attack_speed interval from JSON (e.g., every 1.5s)
- [ ] Ranged units: attack at range, projectile travels to target (visual only, hit is instant calculation)
- [ ] Ranged units have minimum range (cannot attack adjacent units)
- [ ] Rock-paper-scissors bonus: +50% damage for favorable matchup (configurable in JSON)
- [ ] Buildings take 80% reduced damage from non-siege units (multiplier in JSON)
- [ ] Unit plays attack animation during combat
- [ ] Damage numbers float above target (optional, toggle in settings)
- [ ] Unit death triggers death animation → corpse fade → removal
- [ ] Combat state serializable (current target, attack cooldown timer)
- [ ] Unit tests for damage formula with various stat combinations
- [ ] Unit tests for bonus damage rock-paper-scissors matrix
- [ ] Unit tests for building damage reduction","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:34Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:59:56Z","labels":["combat","damage","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-636.3","title":"AI military decision-making — army composition and attack timing","description":"AI builds military units, forms armies, and decides when/where to attack.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] AI builds counter-units based on scouted enemy composition (rock-paper-scissors)
- [ ] Army threshold before attacking loaded from JSON per difficulty
- [ ] AI targets weakest enemy expansion or undefended resource lines
- [ ] AI retreats damaged units below configurable HP threshold

{"id":"roots-of-reason-ovr.7","title":"Balance river access in starting location selection","description":"Extend the starting location fairness algorithm to account for river access.

When selecting starting locations, the existing fairness system ensures balanced resource access and minimum distance. Add river access as a fairness dimension:

Scoring criteria for starting locations:
- River proximity: distance from starting position to nearest river tile (lower = better)
- River direction: does the nearest river flow TOWARD the starting position? (flow toward = high value, flow away = low value)
- River resource access: how many resource nodes (gold, stone) are within 5 tiles of the river AND within 15 tiles of the starting position? (more = better)

Fairness constraint: the river access score variance across all starting positions must be below a threshold. If one player has a river flowing toward their base past 3 gold mines, other players should have comparable river advantages.

If perfect fairness is not achievable with the generated map, the generator should retry river generation (up to 3 attempts) or adjust river paths slightly to improve balance.

Edge cases:
- Island/archipelago maps: rivers may not exist on small islands. Skip river fairness for water-heavy maps.
- Desert-heavy maps: fewer rivers. Relax fairness thresholds when total river count is low.
- If a map has no rivers (rare), skip this entirely.

Acceptance criteria:
- Starting locations have comparable river access scores
- River direction relative to start position is factored into fairness
- Resource nodes near rivers are counted in fairness assessment
- Generator retries or adjusts if river balance is too unfair
- Works correctly on all map sizes and types","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:01:21Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:01:21Z","labels":["balance","map","river","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-ovr.8","title":"Wolf pack spawning during map generation","description":"Spawn wolf packs as neutral Gaia fauna during procedural map generation.

Spawn rules:
- 3-5 wolf packs per map (scale with map size: 3 for small, 4 for medium, 5 for large)
- Each pack contains 2-3 wolves
- Spawn near forest terrain (within 3 tiles of forest)
- Minimum 12 tiles from any player starting position (wolves shouldn't rush players at game start)
- Minimum 8 tiles between wolf pack spawn points (spread them across the map)
- At least 1 wolf pack should be roughly equidistant between pairs of players (contested wolves)
- Wolves do NOT spawn on water, mountain, or desert tiles — forests and grassland near forests only

Wolf entity initial state:
- Owner: Gaia (neutral faction, player_id = -1)
- Behavior: patrol (wander within 8-tile radius of spawn point)
- Aggro: hostile to lone villagers within 3 tiles
- Domestication_progress: 0.0 (float, 0.0 to 1.0)
- Fed_by: null (player_id of last player to feed)

Add wolves to the fauna data model alongside deer:
data/fauna/wolf.json:

  "name": "Wolf",

  "domestication_decay_interval": 60
}

Placeholder sprite: gray circle with pointed ears (triangle on top), slightly larger than villager placeholder. Distinct from deer (brown) and dogs (lighter gray with collar indicator).

Acceptance criteria:
- Maps generate with 3-5 wolf packs in appropriate locations
- Wolves visible and distinct from other fauna
- Wolf packs spread across map, not clustered
- Starting positions safe from immediate wolf aggro
- At least one pack in a contestable position between players","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:05:57Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:05:57Z","labels":["fauna","map","story","wolves"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-2hj.6","title":"Wolf fauna AI: patrol, aggro, and flee behavior","description":"Implement wolf AI behavior as a Gaia fauna unit.

Wolf behavior states (simple state machine):

PATROL (default):
- Wander randomly within 8-tile radius of spawn point
- Pick a random tile in radius, walk to it, idle 3-5 seconds, pick another
- If a lone villager enters aggro_radius (3 tiles): transition to ATTACK
- If 3+ military units enter 5-tile radius: transition to FLEE
- Wolves in the same pack stay loosely grouped (pack cohesion: try to stay within 4 tiles of packmates)

ATTACK:
- Target the nearest villager within aggro radius
- Chase at speed 2.0 (faster than villager at 1.5)
- Attack with 8 damage on contact
- If target dies or moves beyond 6 tiles: return to PATROL
- If 2+ military units approach within 4 tiles: transition to FLEE
- Pack behavior: if one wolf attacks, nearby packmates also aggro the same target

FLEE:
- Run away from the threat (opposite direction) at speed 2.0
- Flee for 5 seconds or until 10 tiles from threat, then return to PATROL
- Flee toward the pack's spawn point (run home)

BEING_FED:
- Entered when a villager uses the Feed command (see domestication story)
- Wolf stops moving, plays eating animation (or idle facing the villager)
- Cannot be aggro'd while being fed
- After feeding completes, return to PATROL (with updated domestication progress)
- If villager is killed during feeding: return to PATROL immediately

DOMESTICATED:
- Terminal state — wolf transforms into Dog unit (see dog story)
- Remove wolf from Gaia faction, create Dog unit for the feeding player

Wolf death:
- When killed: drop 50 Food as a harvestable food source (like hunting a deer)
- Death animation, corpse fades
- Pack members do NOT avenge — they flee when a packmate is killed

Acceptance criteria:
- Wolves patrol naturally around spawn point in loose packs
- Wolves aggro lone villagers and chase them
- Wolves flee from military groups
- Pack behavior: aggro spreads to packmates, flee when packmate dies
- Smooth state transitions with no flickering between states
- Being_fed state correctly pauses aggro","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:05:57Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:05:57Z","labels":["fauna","story","units","wolves"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-ovr.6","title":"River generation with flow direction and hydrology","description":"Generate rivers during procedural map creation. Rivers flow from high elevation to low elevation with encoded flow direction per tile.

Generation algorithm:
1. After terrain elevation is generated, identify watershed sources: tiles at local elevation maxima (mountains, large hills) with elevation > threshold
2. For each source, trace a path downhill following steepest gradient neighbor
3. When two paths meet the same tile, merge into one river (wider)
4. Terminate when reaching ocean, lake, or map edge
5. Assign flow_direction (Vector2i) to each river tile pointing downstream
6. Assign river_id to group tiles belonging to the same river system
7. Set river_width: 1 tile for headwaters/streams, 2 tiles for rivers after a merge or after N tiles from source

River tile data model:
- terrain_type: 'river' (distinct from 'ocean' and 'lake')
- flow_direction: Vector2i — one of 8 directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW)
- river_id: int — which river system
- river_width: 1 or 2

Generation constraints:
- Each map (small/medium/large) should have 2-4 rivers
- Rivers should not start or end within 8 tiles of a player starting position (avoid giving one player an unfair river advantage at spawn)
- At least 1 river should pass within 10 tiles of each starting position (everyone gets river access)
- Rivers must be continuous — no gaps in the tile path
- Rivers should not run perfectly straight — add slight noise to the path for natural appearance
- Rivers can flow through forest (clearing a 1-tile path) but not through mountains

Movement rules for river tiles:
- Land units CAN cross river tiles at 50% movement speed (fording)
- Naval units CAN traverse river tiles (rivers are navigable)
- Buildings CANNOT be placed on river tiles (but River Dock can be placed adjacent)

Visualization:
- River tiles use a distinct blue shade (#4A90C4) different from ocean (#2E5B8A) and lake (#5B9BD5)
- Subtle animated flow indicator: small chevrons or ripples moving in flow_direction
- 2-wide rivers render as a wider water channel

Add river tiles to the procedural tile generator (Phase 1 placeholder: light blue diamond with arrow indicating flow direction).

Acceptance criteria:
- Generated maps contain 2-4 rivers with visible flow direction
- Rivers flow continuously from source to terminus
- Flow direction is consistent and always points downstream
- River tiles are traversable by land (slowly) and naval units
- Starting positions have balanced river access
- River data (flow_direction, river_id) accessible to other systems","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:01:20Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-23T12:18:11Z","closed_at":"2026-02-23T12:18:11Z","close_reason":"Completed in PR #28","labels":["map","river","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fk5.8","title":"Knowledge burning: visual effects, notifications, and audio","description":"Implement the dramatic visual and audio feedback when tech regression occurs.

Visual effects at the destroyed TC location:
- "Burning scroll" particle effect: orange/amber paper fragments rising from the TC ruins
- Particle duration: 3 seconds, rising upward and fading
- Small golden glow that intensifies then fades at the ruin site
- Phase 1 placeholder: amber circle expanding from TC position and fading

Screen effects for the affected player:
- Brief amber/red screen flash (0.3 seconds, 20% opacity overlay)
- Screen shake: subtle (2px amplitude, 0.5 seconds)
- These only show for the player who LOST the tech (not the attacker)

Notifications:
- Defending player: large center-screen notification "Knowledge Lost: [Tech Name]" with the tech icon, fades after 4 seconds
- Attacking player: notification "Enemy knowledge destroyed: [Tech Name]" (smaller, in notification feed)
- All other players: event log entry "[Player] lost knowledge of [Tech Name]"
- Notification color: amber/gold (distinct from red damage notifications and green positive notifications)

Audio:
- "Knowledge lost" sound: a deep resonant chime combined with a paper-burning crackle
- Plays for the affected player at high priority (interrupts other game sounds briefly)
- Attacking player hears a softer "triumph" variant

Tech tree UI reaction:
- If the tech tree viewer is open, the lost tech un-highlights with a "burning away" animation
- The tech node briefly glows amber, then reverts to its "available but not researched" state
- If the tech tree is closed, the un-highlight happens silently (will show correctly when opened)

Minimap indicator:
- Brief amber flash at the location of the destroyed TC on the minimap
- Lasts 2 seconds

Chat/event log:
- Timestamped entry: "[12:45] Your city was sacked! Lost: Iron Casting (+2 Infantry Attack)"
- Include what the tech did so the player understands the impact

Acceptance criteria:
- Burning scroll particles visible at destroyed TC
- Screen flash and shake feel impactful but not disorienting
- All three notification tiers work (defender, attacker, observers)
- Audio cue is distinctive and immediately recognizable
- Tech tree UI correctly un-highlights the lost tech
- Event log entry includes tech name and effect description
- All visuals use Phase 1 placeholder approach (simple shapes/colors, no art required)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:37Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:37Z","labels":["combat","knowledge-burning","story","ui"]}

AGI Core completion triggers Singularity win condition. Each step has high Knowledge cost.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:33Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:33Z","labels":["singularity","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-fxt.7","title":"Tech regression interaction with Singularity victory path","description":"Define and implement how tech regression interacts with the Singularity win condition chain.

The Singularity path (GPU Foundry → Transformer Architecture → AGI Core) represents the highest-value techs in the game. Losing them to TC destruction should be devastating but recoverable.

Interactions:
1. Transformer Architecture is a researched tech — it CAN be lost via regression if it is the most recently researched tech. If lost:
   - AGI Core cannot be constructed (requires Transformer Architecture)
   - If AGI Core is currently under construction: construction PAUSES (not cancelled, not destroyed — just paused, resumable after re-research)
   - Re-research cost: full (extremely expensive in Knowledge)
   - This can delay a Singularity victory by several minutes of game time

2. GPU Foundry and AGI Core are BUILDINGS, not techs. They are not directly affected by tech regression. However:
   - If the tech that unlocked GPU Foundry is lost, the existing GPU Foundry remains but you cannot build another
   - AGI Core depends on Transformer Architecture — if that tech is lost, AGI Core construction pauses

3. Research order strategy:
   - Savvy players will research Transformer Architecture early in their tech queue, then research other techs after it
   - This pushes Transformer Architecture deeper in the history stack, making it less likely to be the "most recent" tech lost
   - Players who rush Singularity techs last are more vulnerable to regression

4. Defense escalation in late game:
   - When a player researches Transformer Architecture, all opponents receive an alert: "[Player] has researched Transformer Architecture" (public knowledge event, like Wonder construction)
   - This signals that the Singularity push has begun, incentivizing others to target that player's TCs
   - The tech leader must invest heavily in defense to protect their research investment
   - This creates a natural "all vs leader" dynamic in the late game

5. UI indicator for Singularity vulnerability:
   - When a player has Singularity-chain techs, their TC info panel shows a "Knowledge at Risk" warning
   - Lists which Singularity tech would be lost if this TC falls
   - Encourages the player to build defenses

Data considerations:
- Tag Singularity-chain techs in tech data: "singularity_chain": true
- These techs trigger the public alert when researched
- AGI Core construction checks for Transformer Architecture status on every tick (not just on start)

Acceptance criteria:
- Transformer Architecture can be lost via regression, pausing AGI Core construction
- AGI Core construction resumes automatically when Transformer Architecture is re-researched
- Public alert fires when Singularity-chain techs are researched
- Tech research order creates meaningful strategic depth (research Singularity techs early to protect them)
- UI shows "Knowledge at Risk" warning on TCs when Singularity techs are researched
- Integration test: destroy TC, lose Transformer Architecture, verify AGI Core pauses, re-research, verify resume","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:38Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:38Z","labels":["knowledge-burning","singularity","story","tech"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-636.1","title":"AI personality types — Builder, Rusher, Boomer, Turtle","description":"Distinct AI personalities that create varied opponent experiences.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] 4 personality types defined in data/ai/personalities.json
- [ ] Builder: prioritizes economy and age advancement, attacks late
- [ ] Rusher: early military pressure, sacrifices economy
- [ ] Boomer: fast expansion to multiple TCs, greedy economy
- [ ] Turtle: walls and defensive buildings, strong late-game army
- [ ] Personality modifies build order selection and military thresholds
- [ ] Each personality has a name and flavor text for game setup screen
- [ ] Personality selection per AI slot in game setup (or random)
- [ ] All personality parameters data-driven from JSON","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dave@mainstreetlogic.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:44:37Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T22:57:26Z","labels":["ai","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-636.6","title":"AI: factor tech regression risk into Town Center defense priority","description":"Adjust AI behavior to account for the Knowledge Burning mechanic.

AI defensive adjustments:
- AI assigns a "tech vulnerability score" to each of its Town Centers based on:
  - Number of researched techs (more techs = more to lose = higher priority to defend)
  - Proximity to enemy units/bases (closer = more vulnerable)
  - Current military presence near the TC (more defenders = less vulnerable)
- AI allocates more military units to defend TCs with high vulnerability scores
- The defensive allocation increases in later ages (more techs at stake)
- Aggressive AI personality still raids more, but maintains a minimum TC garrison proportional to tech count

AI offensive adjustments:
- AI recognizes enemy TCs as high-value targets (not just for territory, but for tech regression)
- When AI has military advantage, it may prioritize TC snipes over unit kills
- If the enemy is ahead in tech, AI increases priority of TC raids as a catch-up mechanism
- Aggressive AI explicitly targets tech-leading opponents' TCs

AI forward TC decision:
- AI evaluates risk/reward of building forward TCs:
  - Benefit: map control, shorter unit reinforcement path
  - Risk: if destroyed, lose a tech
  - Decision weight: build forward TCs only when military advantage is clear, or when the forward position has natural defenses (hills, river, chokepoint)
- Economic AI personality avoids forward TCs (risk-averse)
- Aggressive AI personality builds forward TCs more freely

AI response to own tech loss:
- When AI loses a tech from TC destruction:
  - Increase overall military production priority for 2 minutes (defensive reaction)
  - Re-research the lost tech as soon as resources allow (high priority)
  - If the lost TC was forward: do not rebuild it in the same exposed location

Acceptance criteria:
- AI garrisons TCs proportional to tech count
- AI targets enemy TCs when ahead militarily
- AI avoids building forward TCs when tech regression risk is high
- AI re-researches lost techs promptly
- Different AI personalities (aggressive/economic/balanced) handle the mechanic differently
- AI does not over-prioritize TC defense to the point of never attacking","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"davetashner@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:37Z","created_by":"Dave Tashner","updated_at":"2026-02-22T21:11:37Z","labels":["ai","knowledge-burning","story"]}

{"id":"roots-of-reason-q7z.1","title":"Pirate ship spawning and patrol AI","description":"Implement Gaia pirate ship spawning triggered by Compass research.
